#  Applications of Relaxor AFEs

The energy storage performance of relaxor antiferroelectrics is fundamentally governed by the polarization–electric field (P–E) response. In conventional antiferroelectrics, the long-range ordered AFE state yields a characteristic double P–E loop with large hysteresis loss, limiting both recoverable energy density (W_rec) and efficiency (η). Introducing local compositional disorder—as achieved in the Cd-doped (Pb0.94-xCdxLa0.04)(Hf0.7Sn0.3)O3 system—disrupts this long-range order, producing heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) that dramatically slim the P–E loop (Fig. 1c). The resulting nearly linear, hysteresis-free response directly boosts W_rec and η simultaneously: the Cd3 composition achieves a peak W_rec of ~28 J/cm³ with η exceeding 90% at ~800 kV/cm (Fig. 5b). This improvement stems from two synergistic effects. First, the disordered HDSs suppress the energy barrier for polarization reversal, enabling rapid reversion to the initial state upon field removal and minimizing remnant polarization. Second, the local polarization discontinuities redistribute bound charges and rearrange the internal electric field, delaying polarization saturation and thus enhancing the maximum polarization at high fields. The Weibull analysis (Fig. 5c) further confirms that the breakdown strength (E_b) increases with Cd content, reaching values that support the high applied fields needed for large W_rec. Compared with other Pb-based AFE ceramics, Cd3 occupies a superior W_rec–η space (Fig. 5f), and its W_rec exceeds that of all other ceramics with η > 90% (Fig. 5g). However, the absence of error bars across these performance metrics raises concerns about reproducibility, and the selective benchmarking in Fig. 5g may overstate the absolute leadership claim. Moreover, the Weibull analysis assumes statistical independence of breakdown events, which may not hold if microstructural heterogeneity induces correlated failure paths—a critical point given the central role of HDSs in this material.
